Incorporating stromal cells into a 3D patient-derived model for immuno-oncology drug testing in ovarian cancer

MSc thesis presentation
New Discoveries! –seminar, 25.04.2025 
Aino Elomaa

Supervisors:
Assistant Professor, Anniina Färkkilä, University of Helsinki
Doctoral Researcher, María Hincapié-Otero, University of Helsinki
Professor, Ullamari Pesonen, University of Turku

1

High grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC)

2

 Highlights the need new treatment options

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
most common subtype, accounting 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers
the most aggressive subtype, typically diagnosed at advanced stages with metastasis to omentum and surrounding abdominal organs. 
omentum = large flat adipose tissue layer that covers intraperitoneal organs
first line treatment is surgery and chemotherapy 
despite initial response the treatment, relapse is common
which highlights the need for new treatment options

3

Therapeutic focus in cancer is expanding beyond tumor cells to include the tumor microenvironment (TME)

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
currently the cancer research is shifting the focus from the cancer cell towards the tumor microenvironment to find novel treatments 
The TME refers to a complex and dynamic network of cellular and molecular components that supports tumor growth
All these components as illustrated are essential in shaping the TME and affecting treatment responses

4

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) shape tumor behaviour

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
particularly the cancer-associated fibroblasts affect the tumor behavior
they are highly heterogenous cell population
A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors are linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 
CAFs contribute to tumor growth by interacting with cancer cells, influencing the extracellular matrix remodeling and also modulate immune responses 
as illustrated in this figure
So as CAFs contribute to the treatment responses and in order to create new treatment strategies, a model that represents the TME and especially CAFs is needed

5

Current HGSC models lack non-immune stromal components 

 Incorporating CAFs and endothelial cells enhances the TME-representation

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
The lab has already a pipeline for establishing patient-derived 3D cultures that support the growth of cancer cells and immune cells
however, the model lacks the non-immune stromal components  to enhance the models TME representation and physiological relevance, stromal cells need to be incorporated 
so incoportating CAFs and endothelial cells to this model, is important as then the TME-representative model could be utilized for studying novel treatment approaches and to discover new treatment strategies HGSC

6

Aims of the thesis project included:

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
to reach this goal of TME representative cell culture model, the aims of my thesis were to 
1) establish an optimized tissue digestion pipeline

7

Aims of the thesis project included:

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
2) optimize an enrichment method for CAFs to enable their usage in the future model development, AND

8

Aims of the thesis project included:

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
3) to validate 3D culture conditions for these patient-derived CAFs

9

Figures created with BioRender.com

This project utilized patient-derived HGSC tumor samples

Collected as part of the prospective ONCOSYS-Ova clinical study
Obtained from primary (PDS) and interval debulking surgeries (IDS) at Helsinki University Hospital

PDS: before chemotherapy, IDS: after neoadjuvant chemotherapy

Samples | | 
 | | n=
PDS | | 11
 | Omentum | 8
 | Adnexa | 3
IDS | | 3
 | Omentum | 3
 | | 
In total | | 14

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
before moving to the results
this thesis project utilized HGSC tumor tissue samples 
that where resected from patients enrolled in a prospective ONCOSYS-OVA clinical trial at Helsinki University Hospital
samples were obtained from both primary and interval debulking surgeries 
in total 14 patient-derived tumor sample were used, derived from two anatomical locations, from adnexa and from omentum


Informed written consent was required from each patient before sample acquisition
The use of clinical material was approved by the Ethics Committee of Women’s Clinic, University of Helsinki, and Helsinki Biobank.

10

Optimization of dissociation protocol and analysis on samples cell composition

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
first aim was optimize dissociation protocol and analyze the sample cell composition
fresh resected tumor tissues were dissociated using three different enzymatic dissociation methods: C&H mix, dispase, a sequential treatment with dispase followed by C&H
dispase is neural protease that digest fibronectin and type IV collagen 
C&H digests collagen and hyaluronic acid which are the two key components of extracellular matrix

11

Optimization of dissociation protocol and analysis on samples cell composition

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
to compare the dissociation methods, cell recovery and viability were assessed 
dissociation efficiency was evaluated by quantifying the different cell populations obtained from each protocol using multicolor flow cytometry
cancer cell, leukocyte, CAF and endothelial cell amounts were quantified

12

A) No statistically significant difference in live cell yield between the dissociation protocols

B) Statistically significant difference in cell viability between dispase and sequential protocols

Sequential protocol showed similar or improved cell yield and viability

Wilcoxon signed-rank test for paired sample
* p < 0.05
ns p ≥ 0.05

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
first the cell count and viability between dissociation protocols was evaluated 
mean live cell count show no statistically significant differences between dissociation 
the sequential showed increased viability compared to the dispase

13

FAP (fibroblast activation protein) is commonly used CAF marker

Three additional CAF markers:
α-SMA 
CD29
PDGFR-β
showed similar results

Sequential protocol improved the yield of CAFs

Wilcoxon signed-rank test for paired sample
*** p < 0.001

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
when evaluating the yield of CAF population between the dissociation protocols, the sequential method resulted in the heights yield of CAF yield 
plot illustrates the frequency of FAP+ cells between dissociation protocols
also the flow cytometry panel used included three other markers that can be used to characterize CAFs, a-SMA, CD29 and PDGFR-b all showed similar results 


FAP membrane-bound serine protease highly expressed in activated fibroblast
α-SMA a cytoskeletal protein, is primarily associated with myoCAFs (ECM production and remodeling, characterized by enhanced contractility)
CD29 a transmembrane receptor involved in adhesion, survival, and migration, not specific to CAFs but contributes to CAF-ECM interaction
PDGFR-β is a receptor tyrosine kinase important for fibroblast proliferation, differentiation, and survival.

14

Wilcoxon signed-rank test for paired sample
* p < 0.05,** p < 0.01,
*** p < 0.001, ns p ≥ 0.05

Dissociation protocol influenced the recovered cell composition

A) Sequential protocol improved endothelial cell yield

B) Dispase-based protocol optimized cancer cell recovery

C) Leukocyte recovery remained consistent across protocols

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
also took a look of the other cell populations in the obtained cell suspensions between the dissociation protocols
endothelial cell population showed similar results as CAFs and sequential dissociation protocol improved the yield of endothelial cells
on the other hand, the yield of cancer cell was significantly low with sequential method and dispase dissociations was proven to be most effective in isolating cancer cells
leukocyte populations remained unaffected between the dissociation protocols

15

Future model development will utilize cells obtained from different dissociation protocols

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
So based on these results, the future model development will utilize cells obtained from different dissociation protocols
the sequential protocol is optimal for CAFs and endothelial cells 
on the other hand in previous studies, it was shown that C&H as enzymatic dissociation increase the expressions of stress response genes of cancer and immune cells. 
Therefore cancer and immune cells will be obtained with the dispase dissociation 
as cell suspension obtained with the sequential method contains other cell populations in addition to CAFs CAFs needs to be enriched from the cell suspension

16

Optimization of CAF enrichment protocol

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
Next, as the second aim, a magnetic-activated cell sorting was optimized 
Two approaches were evaluated: 1) positive selection using anti-fibroblast beads, and 2) negative selection through depletion of cancer cells and leukocytes using a combination of anti-CD45 and anti-EpCAM beads. 
In both cases, cell recovery and viability were assessed, and enrichment efficiency was evaluated by multicolor flow cytometry.

17

ns p ≥ 0.05

CAF enrichment via negative selection maximized CAF yield while minimizing contamination

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
when comparing the enrichment methods, no statistically significant differences in mean live cell count was seen
but then comparing the cell proportions within the three samples, we can see that the orange bar illustrating the CAFs is largest with the negative selection
so the negative selection showed maximized CAF yield but also minimized contamination from other cell population

Validation of 3D culture conditions for CAFs

18

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
lastly as the third aim, the aim was to see if the cells can be grown in culture after all the dissociation and enrichment process 
CAF growth and behavior were evaluated under different culture conditions: 
human omentum-derived gel, OmGel, which is produced from HGSC patients tumor-free omental tissue
Commercially available basement membrane extract BME
Suspension culture using ultra low-adhesion plates that supports the formation of a spheroid
and the CAF growth was monitored using light microscopy

19

Scale bar = 1 mm

All culture conditions supported spheroid formation

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
all culture condition supported spheroid formation
but the choice of culture conditions effects the cell spheroid morphology
Suspension culture requires minimal setup but lack the ECM 
BME or more specifically commercially available Cultrex is a BME matrix 
that is derived from mouse sarcoma cells. 
It consists of key ECM proteins and is widely used in 3D cell culture. 
But as it is mouse derived, it not the most physiologically relevant option. 
when increasing the BME matrix concentration, the spheroid stability is compromised
OmGel as human derived matrix 
offers the most physiologically relevant but also requires most effort 
Overall, it was validated that the patient-derived CAFs can be cultured within these conditions, but the key differences between the two matrices lie in their composition and the level of manual work required.


BME = composed of laminin, collagen type IV, entactin, and heparan sulfate proteoglycans

20

Optimized dissociation and enrichment protocols enabled robust CAF recovery for HGSC model development.
Sequential dissociation protocol ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations.
All tested culture conditions supported CAF spheroid formation, though matrix choice remains critical for future model optimization.

 This thesis paves the way for developing a more TME-representative HGSC model, with predictive potential for therapeutic development.

Key outcomes of this thesis

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
First, I optimized both dissociation and enrichment protocols to allow for robust recovery of CAFs from HGSC patient samples. This was a key step in building a foundation for downstream model development.
Using a sequential dissociation approach, I ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations 
Then, I evaluated the three different culture conditions. While all supported the spheroid formation and growth, the choice of matrix is aa critical factor for future model development. 
Overall, these findings lay the groundwork for establishing a TME-representative model of HGSC. This novel model has predictive potential for disease modeling and could serve as a valuable platform for testing new treatment strategies.

Thank you!

21

22

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

23

EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs

Speaker notes:
First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.
Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.
From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.
Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.
This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results
